Oil GWAS prep

library(here)

snps <- read_delim(
  here(
    "raw_data",
    "SoyNAM  4312 SNP genotypes Wm82.a2"
  ),
  na = "-"
) %>% 
  select(-dbSNP_ID,-Chr_Wm82.a2,-Position_Wm82.a2) %>% 
  column_to_rownames(var = "DEPRECATED-BARCSOYNAMSNP6K_ID")

dosage<-snps %>%
  mutate(across(.cols=everything(),
                ~ ifelse(.x=="A","0",
                         ifelse(.x=="H","1",
                                ifelse(.x=="B","2",.x))))) 
sort(unique(unlist(snps)))
sort(unique(unlist(dosage)))
any(is.na(dosage))
sum(is.na(dosage))

# seems there are only 0,1,2 values and missing 
# which is what we want

dosage<-dosage %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(),as.numeric)) %>% 
  as.matrix %>% 
  t()


library(ASRgenomics)

qc <- qc.filtering(M=dosage,
                   maf         = 0.05, # keep markers with MAF >= 0.01
                   marker.call = 0.95, # keep SNPs called in >= 95% of indivs
                   ind.call    = 0.80, # keep individuals with >= 80% SNPs called
                   imput       = TRUE) # simple mean imputation

dosage<-qc$M.clean


Phenotypic data cleaning

oil_pheno<-wholedata %>%
  select(-Yld, -Protein) %>%
  drop_na %>%
  filter(Strain %in% rownames(dosage))

dosage<-dosage[rownames(dosage) %in% oil_pheno$Strain,]

oil_pheno<-oil_pheno %>% 
  arrange(Env) %>% 
  mutate(Env=factor(Env),
         Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(dosage)))

library(asreml)
asr_oil<-asreml(Oil ~ Env,
                 random = ~Strain ,
                 residual = ~dsum(~units|Env),
                 data = oil_pheno)

### extract blups
oilblups<-summary(asr_oil,coef=T)$coef.random  %>%
  # confert to data.frame
  as.data.frame %>%
  # change the rownames to a column the the data.frame
  rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
  # filter to only the BLUPs for the entry main-effect
  # (output includes all random terms)
  filter(grepl("^Strain_",Strain)) %>%
  # for good measure, tidy data,
  # need to remove a prefix that asreml added to each entry name
  mutate(Strain=gsub("Strain_","",Strain,fixed = T)) %>%
  select(-z.ratio, -std.error) %>%
  relocate(last_col(), .before = 1) %>%
  rename(Oil = solution)


ggsnps_filt_centered<-scale(dosage,scale = F, center = T)

oilblups<-oilblups %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain))




Format arguments for GAPIT We then format MyY, MyGD, and MyGM in a way that GAPIT is happy with.

myYo <- oilblups %>%
  transmute(Taxa = as.character(Strain),
            Oil  = as.numeric(Oil)) %>%
  as.data.frame(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# --- genotype for GAPIT ---
# dosage is your final QC'd matrix: rows = taxa, cols = SNPs
stopifnot(is.matrix(dosage))

myGDo <- data.frame(
  Taxa = rownames(dosage),
  dosage,
  check.names = FALSE,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)


# --- map for GAPIT ---
myGMo <- data.frame(
  SNP = colnames(dosage),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE,
  check.names = FALSE
)

tmpo <- strsplit(myGMo$SNP, "_", fixed = TRUE)
myGMo$Chromosome <- as.integer(sub("^Gm", "", vapply(tmpo, `[`, character(1), 1)))
myGMo$Position   <- as.integer(vapply(tmpo, `[`, character(1), 2))

myGMo <- myGMo[, c("SNP", "Chromosome", "Position")]

# --- hard checks ---
stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(myYo$Taxa))
stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(myGDo$Taxa))
stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(myGMo$SNP))

stopifnot(nrow(myGMo) == ncol(myGDo) - 1)
stopifnot(identical(myGMo$SNP, colnames(myGDo)[-1]))

# force same taxa order in Y and GD
keep <- intersect(myYo$Taxa, myGDo$Taxa)
myYo  <- myYo[match(keep, myYo$Taxa), , drop = FALSE]
myGDo <- myGDo[match(keep, myGDo$Taxa), , drop = FALSE]

stopifnot(identical(myYo$Taxa, myGDo$Taxa))



Oil GLM GWAS

library(GAPIT)

oglm <- GAPIT(
  Y = myYo,

title: Genomic Data Analysis Final Project
text:
Conclusions and Biological Significance
For our GWAS analysis on yield, we found 21 total significant SNPS and four significant SNPs across the MLM and MLMM models. The four significant SNPs were For the protein GWAS, the same two SNPs were found using GLM. MLM, and MLMM models. These four SNPs from yield and two SNPs from protein are ideal candidates for further validation and analysis since they were detected in multiple models. After further validation, these SNPs can be integrated into MAS or GS pipelines to improve selection accuracy and genetic gain in soybean breeding programs.
No significant SNPs were detected from any model using oil phenotypic data, and only two SNPs were detected in protein. This could be due to the smaller phenotypic data set that was available for these traits relative to yield or because of the limited size of the SNP data set. The use of 50k chip and the collection of more phenotypic data for these traits would likely provide higher resolution, more accurate results, and identify more significant SNPs.

title: Genomic Data Analysis Final Project
text:
Genomic Selection Background
Genomic prediciton and selection (GS) rely on a variety of statistical approaches to build models and predict phenotypic data for individuals using genotypic data from a training population. The prediction model based on the training population must be generalizeable to later generations in that population to be useful, so multiple models and methods have been adapted to reduce overfitting and accomplish this goal. In brief, n-fold cross validation is performed when the entire population is split into two random groups containing different individuals from the population. One is smaller and contains the test individuals, while the larger group contains the individuals that the model is trained on. The model built on the larger data set is then used to predict the value of the test data set. The predicted values are then compared to the actual values, and the correlation between predicted and observed data produces the prediction accuracy of that model.
Bayesian models have also been used to create test and train populations and determine prediction accuracy. In this part of the project, we create 10-fold G-BLUP cross validation models, genetic relationship matrices, and Bayesian models and determine the ability of these models to predict accuracy for yield, oil, and protein. The models are supplied with phenotypic and genotypic data, and the entire population is broken into a training (80% of data) and test (20% of data) group that is consistently used for all models. GEBVs from the top 10 lines for each trait were extracted from the best performing model, and superior lines will be used in future breeding.

title: Genomic Data Analysis Final Project
text: Data Analysis

title: Genomic Data Analysis Final Project
text:
Yield Models Code
We first downloaded, filtered, and cleaned our genotypic data to be used in the analysis.

library(tidyverse)
library(tidyr)
library(lme4)

library(here)

snps <- read_delim(
  here(
    "raw_data",
    "SoyNAM  4312 SNP genotypes Wm82.a2"
  ),
  na = "-"
) %>% 
  select(-dbSNP_ID,-Chr_Wm82.a2,-Position_Wm82.a2) %>% 
  column_to_rownames(var = "DEPRECATED-BARCSOYNAMSNP6K_ID")

dosage<-snps %>%
  mutate(across(.cols=everything(),
                ~ ifelse(.x=="A","0",
                         ifelse(.x=="H","1",
                                ifelse(.x=="B","2",.x))))) 

sort(unique(unlist(snps)))
sort(unique(unlist(dosage)))
any(is.na(dosage))
sum(is.na(dosage))

# seems there are only 0,1,2 values and missing 
# which is what we want

dosage<-dosage %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(),as.numeric)) %>% 
  as.matrix %>% 
  t()

library(ASRgenomics)

qc <- qc.filtering(M=dosage,
                   maf         = 0.05, # keep markers with MAF >= 0.01
                   marker.call = 0.95, # keep SNPs called in >= 95% of indivs
                   ind.call    = 0.80, # keep individuals with >= 80% SNPs called
                   imput       = TRUE) # simple mean imputation

## Used code below to clean matrix to whole SNP values of 0, 1, or 2. The GS models seemed to work much better when this was done.


gen <- qc$M[, -1]

gen_mat <- as.matrix(qc$M)
mode(gen_mat) <- "numeric"

gen_mat <- round(gen_mat)
gen_mat[gen_mat < 0] <- 0
gen_mat[gen_mat > 2] <- 2


all(is.na(gen_mat) | gen_mat %in% c(0,1,2))

dosage<-gen_mat


Phenotypic data and BLUE extraction
Since we don’t want to BLUP twice, we extracted BLUEs for each strain using asreml and Strain as a fixed effect.

library(here)

wholedata <- read.csv(
  here("raw_data", "trimmedpheno.csv"),
  na.strings = c(NA, ".")
)

yld_pheno<-wholedata %>%
  select(-Oil, -Protein) %>%
  drop_na %>%
  filter(Strain %in% rownames(dosage))

dosage<-dosage[rownames(dosage) %in% yld_pheno$Strain,]

yld_pheno<-yld_pheno %>% 
  arrange(Env) %>% 
  mutate(Env=factor(Env),
         Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(dosage)))


library(asreml)

asr_yldblue<-asreml(Yld ~ Env + Strain ,
                    residual = ~dsum(~units|Env),
                    data = yld_pheno,
                    maxiter = 15,
                    workspace = "800mb")

### Extract BLUEs

yldblues <- predict(asr_yldblue, classify="Strain", workspace = "800mb", pworkspace = "2000mb")$pvals

yldblues<-yldblues %>% rename(Yld = predicted.value) %>% select(-std.error, -status)

yld_pheno<-yldblues


ggsnps_filt_centered<-scale(dosage,scale = F, center = T)


library("sommer")

yld_pheno<-yld_pheno %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain))

###Filtering and expanding marker matrix We tehn made a marker matrix that could be used in RR-BLUP.

# Re-order the marker matrix to match genotype levels in the data
M<-ggsnps_filt_centered[levels(yld_pheno$Strain),]

##hard check
all(rownames(M) == levels(yld_pheno$Strain))


Z <- model.matrix(~ 0 + Strain, data = yld_pheno)

# Make expanded marker matrix for observation-level RR-BLUP
ZM <- Z %*% M

###RR-BLUP We then used our data and marker matrix to fit a ridge-regression BLUP. We also looked at the distribution of SNP effect sizes, extracted GEBVs, and assessed the predictive ability of the model.

library(conflicted)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)

## rrBLUP
fit_rry <- mmes(Yld~1,
                random = ~vsm(ism(ZM), buildGu = FALSE), 
                nIters = 5, 
                # putting buildGu=FALSE is part of the secret sauce 
                ## avoiding a seriously computational burden involving matrices of dimension p by p.
                data=yld_pheno)

### SNP effects

snp_effects_table<-fit_rry$u %>% 
  as.data.frame %>% 
  rownames_to_column(var = "SNP_ID") %>% 
  separate(col = SNP_ID,
           into = c("Chr","Pos","Allele1","Allele2"),
           sep = "_",remove = F) %>% 
  mutate(Pos=as.numeric(Pos),
         Chr=factor(Chr,levels=1:18)) %>% 
  rename(SNPeffect=V1)

fit_rry$u %>% hist(.,main="Distribution of SNP-effects predicted by RR-BLUP")

## hard checks
table(colnames(ggsnps_filt_centered)==rownames(fit_rry$u))

all(colnames(ggsnps_filt_centered) == rownames(fit_rry$u))

### extract GEBVs by multiply SNP matrix against SNP-effects
gy<-ggsnps_filt_centered %*% fit_rry$u

dim(gy)


gy %>% head

summary(gy %>% as.vector)

### Prediction ability

cor(gy[as.character(yld_pheno$Strain), 1], yld_pheno$Yld, use='complete.obs')

###GBLUP We then made a matrix that was recoded as -1, 0, and 1 to work in the sommer package, added a ridge, and ran a GBLUP model using asreml.

### GGBLUP

A<-A.mat(dosage-1)

summary(diag(A))

dim(A)

library(matrixcalc)

##added ridge to GRM
A<-A+diag(1e-6,nrow(A))
is.positive.definite(A)

yld_pheno<-yld_pheno %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(A)))

fit_gblupy<-asreml(fixed     = Yld~1,
                   random    = ~vm(Strain,A),
                   data      = yld_pheno,
                   maxiter = 15,
                   workspace = "800mb")


gblupy <- summary(fit_gblupy, coef = TRUE)$coef.random

gblup_dfy <- summary(fit_gblupy, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
  mutate(Strain = sub("^vm\\(Strain, A\\)_", "", Strain)) %>%
  rename(GEBV = solution)

dfy <- merge(yld_pheno, gblup_dfy, by = "Strain")

cor(dfy$Yld, dfy$GEBV, use = "complete.obs")



Basic Cross-validation
After verifying that the model accuracy was as expected in the basic models, we moved on to a simple cross validation in which the data was split into a training data set containing 80% of the total data, and a test set containing 20% of the data.

## split data and create test and train data sets
strains<-unique(as.character(yld_pheno$Strain))
set.seed(123);
test_strainsy<-sample(strains,size = 0.2*length(strains),replace = F)
train_strainsy<-strains[!strains %in% test_strainso]

# Subset the phenos to only training set
train_phenosy<-yld_pheno %>% 
  filter(Strain %in% train_strainsy) %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(A)))
# Notice we aren't going to modify the kinship matrix A
# It still contains ALL individuals
# And while only 4/5 of individuals are in training phenos, 
# we assigned levels=rownames(A)
## Internally, asreml will know to construct a model predicting all levels

trn_mody<-asreml(fixed     = Yld~1,
                 random    = ~vm(Strain,A),
                 data      = train_phenosy,
                 maxiter = 15,
                 workspace = "800mb")

tst_gebvsy<-summary(trn_mody,coef=T)$coef.random %>% 
  as.data.frame %>% 
  rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>% 
  mutate(Strain=gsub("vm(Strain, A)_","",Strain,fixed = T)) %>% 
  filter(Strain %in% test_strainsy)


tst_gebvsy<-tst_gebvsy %>% 
  left_join(yld_pheno %>% 
              filter(Strain %in% test_strainsy) %>% 
              dplyr::select(Strain,Yld))


cor(tst_gebvsy$solution,tst_gebvsy$Yld)

###Simple Data Split and 5 fold cross validation

### single cross validation step to verify model runs

yldblups<-yld_pheno

library(tidyverse)
library(rsample)
# First, let's set a few parameters
## These will become input arguments to a function we will build
seed<-123; # for repeatability
nrepeats<-1 # number of iterations
nfolds<-5 # number of folds for k-fold cross-validation
strains<-unique(yld_pheno$Strain)
blups<-yld_pheno
trait<-"Yld"


require(rsample)

# Trick to make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
# The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
set.seed(seed); 
seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)

# Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                  seeds=seeds)

# For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
#### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
    set.seed(seeds);
    cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
    return(cvfolds)}))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  unnest(splits)

# Watch what happens if we use the rsample functions "training()" and "testing()"
training(cvsamples$splits[[1]])
testing(cvsamples$splits[[1]])


data<-blups
splits<-cvsamples$splits[[1]]
# --------------

crossVal_onesplito <- function(splits, data, trait){
  # Subset the data to training individuals only
  # Use the training() rsample function 
  trainingdata <- data %>%
    dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(A)))
  # We're doing a GBLUP model.
  # You can set-up whatever you want though.
  fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
              random    = ~vm(Strain,A),
              data      = trainingdata,
              workspace = "800mb")
  # Extract the GEBVs 
  gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, A)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
  # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
  gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
    filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
  # return predictions for testset individuals
  return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
}

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplito(splits = ., 
                                                  data=blups, trait=trait)))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
  unnest(CVresults) %>% 
  unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
  # merge the blups to the predictions
  left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
# Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
#### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
  group_by(repeats) %>% 
  summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))

accuracies

###Entire 10-fold cross validation function and results

crossValy<-function(seed, nrepeats, nfolds, strains, blups, trait){
  
  require(rsample)
  
  # Trick for make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
  # The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
  set.seed(seed); 
  seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)
  
  # Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
  cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                    seeds=seeds)
  
  # For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
  #### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
      set.seed(seeds);
      cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
      return(cvfolds)}))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    unnest(splits)
  
  
  
  crossVal_onesplit <- function(splits, data, trait){
    # Subset the data to training individuals only
    # Use the training() rsample function 
    trainingdata <- data %>%
      dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(A)))
    # We're doing a GBLUP model.
    # You can set-up whatever you want though.
    fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
                random    = ~vm(Strain,A),
                data      = trainingdata,
                workspace = "800mb")
    # Extract the GEBVs 
    gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
      as.data.frame() %>%
      rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, A)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
    # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
    gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
      filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
    # return predictions for testset individuals
    return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
  }
  
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplit(splits = ., 
                                                   data=blups, trait=trait)))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
    unnest(CVresults) %>% 
    unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
    # merge the blups to the predictions
    left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
  # Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
  #### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
  accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
    group_by(repeats) %>% 
    summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))
  
  return(accuracies)
  
}

cvresults<-crossValy(seed=123,nrepeats=3, nfolds=10, 
                     strains=unique(yldblues$Strain), blups=yldblups, trait="Yld")

cvresults



Bayesian mMdels Using BGLR
Finally, we wanted to test multiple Bayesian models to determine which gave the highest prediction accuracy for our data set.

## data splitting and cleaning
strains<-unique(as.character(yld_pheno$Strain))
set.seed(123);
test_strainsy<-sample(strains,size = 0.2*length(strains),replace = F)
train_strainsy<-strains[!strains %in% test_strainsy]

oil_pheno$Strain <- as.character(yld_pheno$Strain)

# Keep only strains with genotypes
common_ids <- intersect(yld_pheno$Strain, rownames(dosage))

#Make sure aligned
yld_pheno <- yld_pheno[yld_pheno$Strain %in% common_ids, ]
dosage <- dosage[common_ids, ]

##Reorder genotype matrix to match phenotype
dosage <- dosage[yld_pheno$Strain, ]

## Create train/test index
train_idx <- which(yld_pheno$Strain %in% train_strainsy)
test_idx  <- which(yld_pheno$Strain %in% test_strainsy)

### Mask test set
y <- yld_pheno$Yld
y[test_idx] <- NA   # THIS is key for BGLR

library(BGLR)

# Build genomic relationship matrix
Z <- scale(dosage, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)
G <- tcrossprod(Z) / ncol(Z)



Bayes GBLUP

fitbgblupy <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(K = G, model = "RKHS")),  # GBLUP via kernel
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000,
  verbose = TRUE
)

### Extract predictions
yhatbgblup <- fitbgblupy$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

bgblupyldac<-cor(yhatbgblup[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
cor(yhatbgblup[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")



Bayes B

fit_bayesBy <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(X = dosage, model = "BayesB")),
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000,
  
)

yhatbb <- fit_bayesBy$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

bbyld<-cor(yhatbb[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
cor(yhatbb[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")

###extract gebvs
gebv_testyldbb <- fit_bayesB$yHat[test_idx]

# get corresponding strain IDs
strain_ids <- rownames(dosage)[test_idx]

# combine into a data frame
gebvs_yldbb <- data.frame(
  Strain = strain_ids,
  GEBV = gebv_testyldbb
)

##sort and extract top 10 stains with GEBVs

library(dplyr)

top10strainsyld <- gebvs_yldbb %>%
  arrange(desc(GEBV)) %>%
  slice(1:10)



Bayes Lasso

fitbly <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(X = dosage, model = "BL")),  # Bayes Lasso
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000
)

yhatbl <- fitbly$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

blyld<-cor(yhatbl[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
cor(yhatbl[test_idx], yld_pheno$Yld[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")

Based on the results of the Bayesian models, we were able to select the model with the highest prediction accuracy and extract the GEBVs produced by that model. The GEBVs were sorted from highest to lowest, and the strains with the highest 10 GEBVs for yield were identified. These strains have both phenotypic data and genotypic data to support their use as parents in future breeding.

library(tidyverse)

library(here)

snps <- read_delim(
  here(
    "raw_data",
    "SoyNAM  4312 SNP genotypes Wm82.a2"
  ),
  na = "-"
) %>% 
  select(-dbSNP_ID,-Chr_Wm82.a2,-Position_Wm82.a2) %>% 
  column_to_rownames(var = "DEPRECATED-BARCSOYNAMSNP6K_ID")

dosage<-snps %>%
  mutate(across(.cols=everything(),
                ~ ifelse(.x=="A","0",
                         ifelse(.x=="H","1",

sort(unique(unlist(snps)))
sort(unique(unlist(dosage)))
any(is.na(dosage))
sum(is.na(dosage))

# seems there are only 0,1,2 values and missing 
# which is what we want

dosage<-dosage %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(),as.numeric)) %>% 
  as.matrix %>% 
  t()

library(ASRgenomics)

qc <- qc.filtering(M=dosage,
                   maf         = 0.05, # keep markers with MAF >= 0.01
                   marker.call = 0.95, # keep SNPs called in >= 95% of indivs
                   ind.call    = 0.80, # keep individuals with >= 80% SNPs called
                   imput       = TRUE) # simple mean imputation

## Used code below to clean matrix to whole SNP values of 0, 1, or 2. The GS models seemed to work much better when this was done.


geno <- qc$M[, -1]

geno_mat <- as.matrix(qc$M)
mode(geno_mat) <- "numeric"

geno_mat <- round(geno_mat)
geno_mat[geno_mat < 0] <- 0
geno_mat[geno_mat > 2] <- 2


all(is.na(geno_mat) | geno_mat %in% c(0,1,2))

dosage<-geno_mat



Phenotypic data and BLUE extraction
Since we don’t want to BLUP twice, we extracted BLUEs for each strain using asreml and Strain as a fixed effect.

library(here)

wholedata <- read.csv(
  here("raw_data", "trimmedpheno.csv"),
  na.strings = c(NA, ".")
)
oil_pheno<-wholedata %>%
  select(-Yld, -Protein) %>%
  drop_na %>%
  filter(Strain %in% rownames(dosage))

dosage<-dosage[rownames(dosage) %in% oil_pheno$Strain,]

oil_pheno<-oil_pheno %>% 
  arrange(Env) %>% 
  mutate(Env=factor(Env),
         Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(dosage)))


library(asreml)

asr_oilblue<-asreml(Oil ~ Env + Strain ,
                    residual = ~dsum(~units|Env),
                    data = oil_pheno,
                    maxiter = 15,
                    workspace = "800mb")

### Extract BLUEs

oilblues <- predict(asr_oilblue, classify="Strain", workspace = "800mb", pworkspace = "2000mb")$pvals

oilblues<-oilblues %>% rename(Oil = predicted.value) %>% select(-std.error, -status)

oil_pheno<-oilblues


ggsnps_filt_centered<-scale(dosage,scale = F, center = T)


library("sommer")

oil_pheno<-oil_pheno %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain))

###Filtering and expanding marker matrix

# Re-order the marker matrix to match genotype levels in the data
M<-ggsnps_filt_centered[levels(oil_pheno$Strain),]

##hard check
all(rownames(M) == levels(oil_pheno$Strain))


Z <- model.matrix(~ 0 + Strain, data = oil_pheno)

# Make expanded marker matrix for observation-level RR-BLUP
ZM <- Z %*% M

###RR-BLUP We then used our data to fit a ridge-regression BLUP

library(conflicted)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)

## rrBLUP
fit_rro <- mmes(Oil~1,
                random = ~vsm(ism(ZM), buildGu = FALSE), 
                nIters = 5, 
                # putting buildGu=FALSE is part of the secret sauce 
                ## avoiding a seriously computational burden involving matrices of dimension p by p.
                data=oil_pheno)

### SNP effects

fit_rro$u %>% hist(.,main="Distribution of SNP-effects predicted by RR-BLUP")

snp_effects_table<-fit_rro$u %>% 
  as.data.frame %>% 
  rownames_to_column(var = "SNP_ID") %>% 
  separate(col = SNP_ID,
           into = c("Chr","Pos","Allele1","Allele2"),
           sep = "_",remove = F) %>% 
  mutate(Pos=as.numeric(Pos),
         Chr=factor(Chr,levels=1:18)) %>% 
  rename(SNPeffect=V1)

## hard checks
table(colnames(ggsnps_filt_centered)==rownames(fit_rro$u))

all(colnames(ggsnps_filt_centered) == rownames(fit_rro$u))

### extract GEBVs by multiply SNP matrix against SNP-effects
go<-ggsnps_filt_centered %*% fit_rro$u

dim(go)


go %>% head

summary(go %>% as.vector)

### Prediction accuracy

cor(go[as.character(oil_pheno$Strain), 1], oil_pheno$Oil, use='complete.obs')

Basic Cross-validation
After verifying that the model accuracy was as expected in the basic models, we moved on to a simple cross validation in which the data was split into a training data set containing 80% of the total data, and a test set containing 20% of the data.

## split data and create test and train data sets
strains<-unique(as.character(oil_pheno$Strain))
set.seed(123);
test_strainso<-sample(strains,size = 0.2*length(strains),replace = F)
train_strainso<-strains[!strains %in% test_strainso]

# Subset the phenos to only training set
train_phenoso<-oil_pheno %>% 
  filter(Strain %in% train_strainso) %>% 
  mutate(Strain=factor(Strain,levels=rownames(Ao)))
# Notice we aren't going to modify the kinship matrix A
# It still contains ALL individuals
# And while only 4/5 of individuals are in training phenos, 
# we assigned levels=rownames(A)
## Internally, asreml will know to construct a model predicting all levels

trn_modo<-asreml(fixed     = Oil~1,
                 random    = ~vm(Strain,Ao),
                 data      = train_phenoso,
                 maxiter = 15,
                 workspace = "800mb")

tst_gebvso<-summary(trn_modo,coef=T)$coef.random %>% 
  as.data.frame %>% 
  rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>% 
  mutate(Strain=gsub("vm(Strain, Ao)_","",Strain,fixed = T)) %>% 
  filter(Strain %in% test_strainso)


tst_gebvso<-tst_gebvso %>% 
  left_join(oil_pheno %>% 
              filter(Strain %in% test_strainso) %>% 
              dplyr::select(Strain,Oil))


cor(tst_gebvso$solution,tst_gebvso$Oil)

library(tidyverse)
library(rsample)
# First, let's set a few parameters
## These will become input arguments to a function we will build
seed<-123; # for repeatability
nrepeats<-1 # number of iterations
nfolds<-5 # number of folds for k-fold cross-validation
strains<-unique(oil_pheno$Strain)
blups<-oil_pheno
trait<-"Oil"


require(rsample)

# Trick to make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
# The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
set.seed(seed); 
seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)

# Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                  seeds=seeds)

# For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
#### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
    set.seed(seeds);
    cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
    return(cvfolds)}))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  unnest(splits)

# Watch what happens if we use the rsample functions "training()" and "testing()"
training(cvsamples$splits[[1]])
testing(cvsamples$splits[[1]])


data<-blups
splits<-cvsamples$splits[[1]]
# --------------

crossVal_onesplito <- function(splits, data, trait){
  # Subset the data to training individuals only
  # Use the training() rsample function 
  trainingdata <- data %>%
    dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(Ao)))
  # We're doing a GBLUP model.
  # You can set-up whatever you want though.
  fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
              random    = ~vm(Strain,Ao),
              data      = trainingdata,
              workspace = "800mb")
  # Extract the GEBVs 
  gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, Ao)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
  # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
  gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
    filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
  # return predictions for testset individuals
  return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
}

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplito(splits = ., 
                                                  data=blups, trait=trait)))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
  unnest(CVresults) %>% 
  unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
  # merge the blups to the predictions
  left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
# Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
#### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
  group_by(repeats) %>% 
  summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))

accuracies

###Entire 10-fold cross validation function and results

crossValo<-function(seed, nrepeats, nfolds, strains, blups, trait){
  
  require(rsample)
  
  # Trick for make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
  # The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
  set.seed(seed); 
  seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)
  
  # Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
  cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                    seeds=seeds)
  
  # For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
  #### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
      set.seed(seeds);
      cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
      return(cvfolds)}))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    unnest(splits)
  
  
  
  crossVal_onesplit <- function(splits, data, trait){
    # Subset the data to training individuals only
    # Use the training() rsample function 
    trainingdata <- data %>%
      dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(Ao)))
    # We're doing a GBLUP model.
    # You can set-up whatever you want though.
    fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
                random    = ~vm(Strain,Ao),
                data      = trainingdata,
                workspace = "800mb")
    # Extract the GEBVs 
    gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
      as.data.frame() %>%
      rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, Ao)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
    # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
    gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
      filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
    # return predictions for testset individuals
    return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
  }
  
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplit(splits = ., 
                                                   data=blups, trait=trait)))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
    unnest(CVresults) %>% 
    unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
    # merge the blups to the predictions
    left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
  # Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
  #### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
  accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
    group_by(repeats) %>% 
    summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))
  
  return(accuracies)
  
}

cvresults<-crossValo(seed=123,nrepeats=3, nfolds=10, 
                     strains=unique(oilblups$Strain), blups=oilblups, trait="Oil")

oiltenfoldacc<-cvresults
oiltenfoldacc

gblup <- summary(fit_gblupp, coef = TRUE)$coef.random

gblup_df <- summary(fit_gblupp, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
  mutate(Strain = sub("^vm\\(Strain, Ap\\)_", "", Strain)) %>%
  rename(GEBV = solution)

df <- merge(prot_pheno, gblup_df, by = "Strain")

cor(df$Protein, df$GEBV, use = "complete.obs")

###Simple Data Split and 5 fold cross validation

### single cross validation step to verify model runs

protblups<-prot_pheno

library(tidyverse)
library(rsample)
# First, let's set a few parameters
## These will become input arguments to a function we will build
seed<-123; # for repeatability
nrepeats<-1 # number of iterations
nfolds<-5 # number of folds for k-fold cross-validation
strains<-unique(prot_pheno$Strain)
blups<-prot_pheno
trait<-"Protein"


require(rsample)

# Trick to make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
# The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
set.seed(seed); 
seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)

# Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                  seeds=seeds)

# For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
#### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
    set.seed(seeds);
    cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
    return(cvfolds)}))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  unnest(splits)

# Watch what happens if we use the rsample functions "training()" and "testing()"
training(cvsamples$splits[[1]])
testing(cvsamples$splits[[1]])


data<-blups
splits<-cvsamples$splits[[1]]
# --------------

crossVal_onesplito <- function(splits, data, trait){
  # Subset the data to training individuals only
  # Use the training() rsample function 
  trainingdata <- data %>%
    dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(Ap)))
  # We're doing a GBLUP model.
  # You can set-up whatever you want though.
  fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
              random    = ~vm(Strain,Ap),
              data      = trainingdata,
              workspace = "800mb")
  # Extract the GEBVs 
  gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
    as.data.frame() %>%
    rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, Ap)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
  # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
  gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
    filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
  # return predictions for testset individuals
  return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
}

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplito(splits = ., 
                                                  data=blups, trait=trait)))

cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
  unnest(CVresults) %>% 
  unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
  # merge the blups to the predictions
  left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
# Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
#### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
  dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
  group_by(repeats) %>% 
  summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))

accuracies

###Entire 10-fold cross validation function and results

crossValo<-function(seed, nrepeats, nfolds, strains, blups, trait){
  
  require(rsample)
  
  # Trick for make the whole set of cross validation repeatable
  # The input "seed" is a master "seed" that makes seeds for the rest of the process
  set.seed(seed); 
  seeds<-sample(1:10000,replace = F,size=nrepeats)
  
  # Make a list of training-testing splits spanning the number of iterations we want to do  
  cvsamples<-tibble(repeats=1:nrepeats,
                    seeds=seeds)
  
  # For each repeat, us the `vfold_cv` function in rsample 
  #### to make train-test splits for k-fold cross-validation   
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(splits=map(seeds,function(seeds,...){
      set.seed(seeds);
      cvfolds<-vfold_cv(tibble(Strain=strains),v = nfolds)
      return(cvfolds)}))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    unnest(splits)
  
  
  
  crossVal_onesplit <- function(splits, data, trait){
    # Subset the data to training individuals only
    # Use the training() rsample function 
    trainingdata <- data %>%
      dplyr::filter(Strain %in% training(splits)$Strain) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = factor(Strain, levels = rownames(Ap)))
    # We're doing a GBLUP model.
    # You can set-up whatever you want though.
    fit<-asreml(fixed     = as.formula(paste0(trait,"~1")),
                random    = ~vm(Strain,Ap),
                data      = trainingdata,
                workspace = "800mb")
    # Extract the GEBVs 
    gpreds <-summary(fit, coef = TRUE)$coef.random %>%
      as.data.frame() %>%
      rownames_to_column(var = "Strain") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Strain = gsub("vm(Strain, Ap)_", "", Strain, fixed = T))
    # Only the test-set's GEBVs are wanted for measuring accuracy
    gpreds_testsetOnly <- gpreds %>%
      filter(Strain %in% testing(splits)$Strain)
    # return predictions for testset individuals
    return(tibble(gpreds_testsetOnly = list(gpreds_testsetOnly)))
  }
  
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    mutate(CVresults=map(splits,~crossVal_onesplit(splits = ., 
                                                   data=blups, trait=trait)))
  
  cvsamples<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(-splits) %>% 
    unnest(CVresults) %>% 
    unnest(gpreds_testsetOnly) %>% 
    # merge the blups to the predictions
    left_join(blups, by = "Strain")
  # Use group_by() + summarize() function to compute the cor(PRED,OBS) 
  #### seperately for each _iteration_ (repeat)
  accuracies<-cvsamples %>% 
    dplyr::select(repeats,id,Strain,solution,trait) %>% 
    group_by(repeats) %>% 
    summarize(accuracy=cor(solution,get(trait),use='complete.obs'))
  
  return(accuracies)
  
}

cvresults<-crossValo(seed=123,nrepeats=3, nfolds=10, 
                     strains=unique(protblups$Strain), blups=protblups, trait="Protein")

cvresults



Bayesian Models Using BGLR
Finally, we wanted to test multiple Bayesian models to determine which gave the highest prediction accuracy for our data set.

## data splitting and cleaning
strains<-unique(as.character(prot_pheno$Strain))
set.seed(123);
test_strainsp<-sample(strains,size = 0.2*length(strains),replace = F)
train_strainsp<-strains[!strains %in% test_strainsp]

prot_pheno$Strain <- as.character(prot_pheno$Strain)

# Keep only strains with genotypes
common_ids <- intersect(prot_pheno$Strain, rownames(dosage))

#Make sure aligned
prot_pheno <- prot_pheno[prot_pheno$Strain %in% common_ids, ]
dosage <- dosage[common_ids, ]

##Reorder genotype matrix to match phenotype
dosage <- dosage[prot_pheno$Strain, ]

## Create train/test index
train_idx <- which(prot_pheno$Strain %in% train_strainsp)
test_idx  <- which(prot_pheno$Strain %in% test_strainsp)

### Mask test set
y <- prot_pheno$Protein
y[test_idx] <- NA   # THIS is key for BGLR

library(BGLR)

# Build genomic relationship matrix
Z <- scale(dosage, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)
G <- tcrossprod(Z) / ncol(Z)



Bayes GBLUP

fitbgblup <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(K = G, model = "RKHS")),  # GBLUP via kernel
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000,
  verbose = TRUE
)

### Extract predictions
yhatbgblup <- fitbgblup$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

bgblupprot<-cor(yhatbgblup[test_idx], prot_pheno$Protein[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
bgblupprot



Bayes B

fit_bayesB <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(X = dosage, model = "BayesB")),
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000,
  
)

yhatbb <- fit_bayesB$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

bbprot<-cor(yhatbb[test_idx], prot_pheno$Protein[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
bbprot



Bayes Lasso

fitbl <- BGLR(
  y = y,
  ETA = list(list(X = dosage, model = "BL")),  # Bayes Lasso
  nIter = 12000,
  burnIn = 2000
)

yhatbl <- fitbl$yHat

### Compute prediction accuracy

blprot<-cor(yhatbl[test_idx], prot_pheno$Protein[test_idx], use = "complete.obs")
blprot

gebv_testprotbl <- fitbl$yHat[test_idx]

# get corresponding strain IDs
strain_ids <- rownames(dosage)[test_idx]

# combine into a data frame
gebvs_protbl <- data.frame(
  Strain = strain_ids,
  GEBV = gebv_testprotbl
)

##sort and extract top 10 stains with GEBVs

library(dplyr)

top10strainsprot <- gebvs_protbl %>%
  arrange(desc(GEBV)) %>%
  slice(1:10)

top10strainsprot

##GS Results Sumary
The models used for genomic prediction of oil and protein had a significantly higher prediction accuracy than those for yield. For oil, BayesB had the highest accuracy out of the four models tested which was 0.684. For protein, Bayes Lasso was the best prediction model with an accuracy of 0.651. The high prediction accuracy obtained by these models suggests that they can be incorporated into a breeding pipeline to make selections based on genotypic markers and predicted GEBVs.This could help breeders make genetic gain more quickly and could help select individual that have high GEBVs for both oil and yield. Selection and breeding of those individuals could help reduce the negative correlation between oil and protein levels in soybean.
The models predicting soybean yield had the lowest prediction accuracy out of the three traits analyzed. The BayesB model produced the highest prediction accuracy for yield out of the four models tested, which was 0.384. This level of accuracy is probably not quite high enough to incorporate into a breeding pipeline; however, increasing the amount of genotypic data for each line via 50k SNP chips could help improve the prediction accuracy to a more useful level.
The lower prediction accuracy for yield is expected because the heritability for yield was lower than oil and protein, and environmental factors typically have a large impact on yield. This means that prediction based solely on genotypic data and GEBVs should be lower for yield.

title: Genomic Data Analysis Final Project
text:
Concluding Remarks
Overall, we found that GWAS and GS could both be implemented successfully in the SoyNAM data sets. In our GWAS analysis, we were able to find SNPs that were significant across multiple models in GAPIT for yield and protein. These SNPs can be incorporated in MAS or GS to help make greater genetic gains in these important traits. The GS models for oil and protein has high prediction accuracy which suggests their utility in future breeding and selection pipelines.
